title: Analytic Inverse Design of Temporal Metamaterials via Space-Time Duality
authors: Giuseppe Castaldi, Marino Coppolaro, Massimo Moccia, Carlo Rizza, Nader Engheta, Vincenzo Galdi
abstract: Temporal metamaterials, created by modulating the refractive index in time, offer powerful means of controlling wave propagation but still lack a systematic design methodology. Here, we develop an analytic inverse-design framework rooted in space-time duality and the established theory of one-dimensional spatial inverse scattering. By prescribing reflection (backward-wave) and transmission (forward-wave) responses in rational-function form, we obtain closed-form refractive-index modulations that are guaranteed to be physically admissible. This approach avoids iterative optimization and provides direct analytic control of the modulation. We illustrate the method with syntheses of mathematical operators, such as derivatives and integrals, as well as Chebyshev- and Butterworth-type filters, and validate the results through finite-difference time-domain simulations. Our findings establish a general route to temporal media with tailored functional and spectral responses, enabling applications in wave-based information processing, programmable filtering, and amplification schemes inspired by photonic time crystals.
summary: This paper introduces a new way to design materials that change their properties over time. By using a mathematical trick called space-time duality, the authors can directly calculate the exact changes needed in a material's refractive index to create specific effects, like filters or signal amplifiers. This avoids the need for slow, trial-and-error computer optimizations. The method was successfully tested to create mathematical operators and frequency filters, providing a blueprint for future programmable wave-based technologies.
main_problem: The lack of a systematic, analytic design methodology for temporal metamaterials to achieve precise, non-iterative control over wave dynamics such as filtering and amplification.
main_result: The development of an analytic inverse-design framework using space-time duality that provides closed-form refractive-index modulations for desired wave responses.
method: The authors map 1D temporal scattering problems onto 1D spatial inverse scattering problems using space-time duality and solve them via rational-function prescription and the Gel'fand-Levitan-Marchenko theory.
model_or_system: A 1D temporal metamaterial where the refractive index is modulated in time, transitioning from an initial value to a final value.
key_observables: Reflection and transmission coefficients, refractive-index profile, electric displacement field distribution, and wavenumber-spectrum.
important_parameters: Initial and final refractive indices (ni, ne), modulation duration, and the static limit (k to 0).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the refractive index remains greater than or equal to 1 and neglects dispersive or lossy effects.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates space-time duality; Figure 2 shows the synthesis of a first-derivative operator; Figure 3 demonstrates a Chebyshev low-pass filter; Figure 4 presents a Butterworth-type amplifying filter.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the design problem, establishes the space-time duality framework, details the analytic inverse-design mathematical method, demonstrates applications via synthesized operators and filters, and validates results using FDTD simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conceptual illustration of the space-time duality. (a), (b) Wave interactions with a spatially inhomoheneous and time-varying medium, respectively. Relevant quantities and details are described in the text.

FIG. 2. (a) FDTD-computed space-time map of normalized electric displacement ﬁeld for a temporal metamaterial syn- thesized to implement a ﬁrst-derivative operation in the re- ﬂection (backward) response, assuming ˆni = ˆne = 3.3. The modulation interval is marked by horizontal black dashed lines, and the inset shows the corresponding time-varying refractive-index proﬁle ˆn(t). (b) Spatial ﬁeld distribution at to = 70T , comparing FDTD simulation results with the theo- retical prediction. The inset displays the modulated-Gaussian wavepacket used as the incident excitation.

FIG. 3. (a) FDTD-computed space-time map of normalized electric displacement ﬁeld for a temporal metamaterial syn- thesized to implement a Chebyshev-type low-pass ﬁlter in the reﬂection (backward-wave) response, assuming ˆni = 1 and ˆne = 3. The modulation interval is marked by horizontal black dashed lines, and the inset shows the corresponding time-varying refractive-index proﬁle ˆn(t). (b) Wavenumber- spectrum (magnitude) of normalized electric displacement ﬁeld at t = 70T , comparing FDTD simulation results (trans- formed via FFT) with the theoretical prediction. The inset displays the incident spectrum and the ﬁlter response.

FIG. 4. (a) FDTD-computed space-time map of normal- ized electric displacement ﬁeld for a temporal metamate- rial synthesized to implement a Butterworth-type narrow- band amplifying ﬁlter in the reﬂection (backward-wave) re- sponse, assuming ˆni = ˆne = 2. The modulation interval is marked by horizontal black dashed lines, and the inset shows the corresponding time-varying refractive-index proﬁle ˆn(t). (b) Wavenumber-spectrum (magnitude) of normalized electric displacement ﬁeld at t = 70T , comparing FDTD simulation results (transformed via FFT) with the theoretical predic- tion. The inset displays the incident spectrum and the ﬁlter response.
